Following removal from the source of the burn, the skin and clothing are still hot, and this heat continues to injure the tissues, causing an increase in burn depth and seriousness of the injury. Cooling halts this process and, if done appropriately, is beneficial. Cooling should be done with tap water or any source of clean room-temperature water, but it should be undertaken for no more than 5 to 10 minutes. Cooling for longer periods of time can induce hypothermia and subsequent shock. Do not use ice or ice water because this may induce hypothermia.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 5-year-old female was struck by a car. She presents with retractions and nasal flaring. The airway is patent, breathing is fast and shallow, and the carotid pulse is weak and slow. Which of the steps in her management should be done first?
questions.explanation: Note whether the child is 'working' to breathe, demonstrated by subcostal or suprasternal retractions, nasal flaring, or grunting. If you have any doubt that the child is breathing adequately on his or her own, immediately assist the child's breathing.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following regarding trauma in the elderly is true?
options: Elderly patients are less likely to bleed internally than younger patients
Elderly patients have a better outcome following burns than younger patients
Fatal outcomes are more likely in the elderly than in the young
Motor vehicle collisions are an uncommon mechanism of injury
questions.explanation: Geriatric patients can die from less severe injuries than younger patients. In addition, it is often difficult to separate the effects of the aging process or of a chronic illness from the consequences of an injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Supine hypotension syndrome in the pregnant patient is caused by:
options: Uterine obstruction of venous blood flow
Uterine pressure on the vagal nerve
questions.explanation: In supine hypotension syndrome, the hypotension is caused by the weight of the pregnant uterus pressing on the inferior vena cava and decreasing the return of blood to the heart by up to 30%.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following has a greater chance of surviving traumatic cardiopulmonary arrest?
options: Patients who suffer blunt force trauma to the torso
Patients with penetrating chest trauma who are hypothermic
questions.explanation: Trauma patients who presented unconscious, without palpable pulse or spontaneous respiration, found that patients with sinus rhythm and nondilated (< 4 mm) reactive pupils had a good chance of survival.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following concerning blast injury is true?
options: Primary blast injury is caused by heat
Secondary blast injury is caused by objects propelled by the explosion
Tertiary blast injury is caused by toxic fumes
Quaternary blast injury is caused by the displacement of the body
questions.explanation: The secondary blast injury is the result of the patient being struck by material (shrapnel) propelled by the blast force.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The purpose of the ITLS Primary Survey is to:
options: Identify all immediate life threats within the first 2 minutes
Determine if the patient has any medical allergies
questions.explanation: The purpose of the initial assessment is to prioritize the patient and to identify immediately life-threatening conditions and take less than 2 minutes.
questions.hint: Primary survey ? find life threats fast
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following would be the most compelling reason to immediately intubate a patient?
questions.explanation: Continued problems with mask ventilation should prompt the consideration of airway obstruction as a potential cause. Ultimately, the placement of a 'rescue' ventilation device (BIAD, such as laryngeal mask airway) or endotracheal tube may be required.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The depth of the ET tube at the teeth should be approximately ___ times the diameter of the tube.
questions.explanation: The mark on the tube that is even with the teeth should be three times the diameter of the ET tube. Thus, an 8.0 mm tube in an adult should be at 24 cm.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: During transport, a trauma patient develops shallow and rapid breathing, cyanosis and a weak, rapid carotid pulse with distended neck veins and diminished breath sounds on the right. You should:
questions.explanation: Decompress the affected side of the chest, if indicated. The indication for performing emergency chest decompression is the presence of a tension pneumothorax with decompensation as evidenced by more than one of the following: respiratory distress and cyanosis, loss of the radial pulse (late shock), decreasing level of consciousness.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 23-year-old female fell from a second-floor balcony. Upon arrival, you find her lying in the grass. She responds to verbal commands and your assessment reveals flat neck veins, and normal chest, abdomen and pelvis examinations. Her skin is cool, clammy and ashen; respirations are rapid and shallow; radial pulses are too rapid to count and thready. You place her on the heart monitor and it shows a wide-complex tachycardia of about 280 per minute. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: If cardiac output falls (either due to dropping or a very fast heart rate or lowered stroke volume), blood pressure will fall.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct orientation for inserting an intraosseous needle to the proximal tibia?
options: Medial to the midline, avoiding the growth plate
Medial to the midline, pointing toward the growth plate
Lateral to the midline, avoiding the growth plate
Lateral to the midline, pointing toward the growth plate
questions.explanation: Approximately 2 cm below the patella and approximately 2 cm (depending on the patient's anatomy) medial to the tibial tuberosity, or one finger breadth medial to the tibial tuberosity. This is often the easiest site to locate.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Barbiturates should not be administered as a sedative for the head-injured patient because they:
questions.explanation: Careful use of benzodiazepines can decrease agitation without dropping blood pressure. An added benefit to the use of benzodiazepines is that they prevent seizures. Seizure prophylaxis in the head injured patient should be initiated on the recommendation of medical direction. Other agents suitable for use include phenytoin. Do not use barbiturates because they can cause hypotension.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: In which situation should an Emergency Rescue be performed?
options: Leaking antifreeze from a vehicle radiator
questions.explanation: Emergency Rescue is reserved for those situations in which there is immediate (within seconds) environmental threat to the life of the victim and/or emergency care responder. Patients should be moved to a safe area in a manner that places the emergency care responder at the least risk. Rapid extrication should be considered for patients whose medical conditions or situations require fast intervention (1 or 2 minutes— but not seconds) to prevent death.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is the most common cause of abdominal trauma?
questions.explanation: Blunt trauma is the most common mechanism of abdominal injury and has relatively high mortality rates of 10% to 30%. The reason is likely related to the frequency of accompanying injuries to the head, chest, pelvis, and/or an extremity in as many as 70% of motor vehicle collision victims.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Proper immobilization of a forearm (radius and ulna) fracture includes splinting the:
questions.explanation: Use a splint that will immobilize one joint above and one joint below the injury site.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 16-year-old female receives 25% partial thickness thermal burns. You should:
options: Clean and cover the burns with an antimicrobial sheet
Clean and apply ice packs to the burned area
Cool the burn and then protect from heat loss
questions.explanation: Limit burn wound progression as much as possible. Rapid cooling early in the course of a surface burn injury can help limit this progression. Following removal from the source of the burn, the skin and clothing are still hot, and this heat continues to injure the tissues, causing an increase in burn depth and seriousness of the injury. Cooling halts this process and, if done appropriately, is beneficial. Cooling should be done with tap water or any source of clean room-temperature water, but it should be undertaken for no more than 5 to 10 minutes. Cooling for longer periods of time can induce hypothermia and subsequent shock. Do not use ice or ice water because this may induce hypothermia.
questions.hint: Burns ? cool then prevent heat loss
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 3-year-old male fell 9 feet (3 meters). He has a decreased level of consciousness and is making persistent 'grunting' sounds with respirations. Your initial treatment is to:
options: Apply a cervical collar and transport immediately
Suction the patient and apply oxygen with a nasal cannula
Provide ventilatory support with supplementary oxygen
Quickly complete a rapid trauma survey
questions.explanation: Note whether the child is 'working' to breathe, demonstrated by subcostal or suprasternal retractions, nasal flaring, or grunting. Look at the chest rise, listen for air going in and out, and feel the air coming out of the nose. If there is no movement, reposition the jaw to remove any anatomical obstruction. If you still do not sense any air exchange, you must breathe for the child. If you have any doubt that the child is breathing adequately on his or her own, immediately assist the child's breathing.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 78-year-old female driver struck a parked car while she was traveling at a low rate of speed. She is complaining of shortness of breath and chest pain. Your assessment and treatment should include:
options: Breath sounds, history, place in a position of comfort
questions.explanation: All elderly patients should have cardiac monitoring, pulse oximetry, and capnography, if available.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When transporting a third trimester pregnant trauma patient in spinal motion restriction, you should:
options: Tilt or rotate the board or patient 15-30 degrees to the left
Manually displace the uterus to the right
Secure the patient in the left lateral recumbent position
questions.explanation: Supine hypotension syndrome: acute hypotension in the pregnant patient due to decreased venous return. It usually occurs when the patient is in a supine position with a 20-week or larger uterus (uterus up to umbilicus). The transport of all pregnant trauma patients, if no contraindication exists, should be by one of the following methods to alleviate vena cava compression: Tilt or rotate the backboard 15 to 30 degrees to the patient's left. Elevate the right hip four to six inches (10 to 15 centimeters) with a towel, and manually displace the uterus to the left.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding SPO2 is correct?
options: An SPO2 reading of 90% is equivalent to a PAO2 reading of 90 mmHg
As a general rule, any pulse oximeter reading below 95% is cause for concern
You should try to maintain a pulse oximeter reading of 95% or higher in your trauma patient
SPO2 is unreliable in trauma patients and has no value in their assessment
questions.explanation: Try to maintain a pulse oximeter reading of 95% or higher. However, do not withhold oxygen from a patient with a pulse oximeter reading above 95% who also shows signs and symptoms of hypoxia or difficulty breathing.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The purpose of the ITLS Primary Survey is to identify:
questions.explanation: The purpose of the initial assessment is to prioritize the patient and to identify immediately life-threatening conditions.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: You are delivering adequate ventilation volume when:
options: You provide 5-7 ml/kg per of ventilator support
Air begins to leak from the mask seal
You observe bilateral chest rise and fall
questions.explanation: Ventilate with enough volume (8-10 ml per kg body weight) to cause adequate chest rise.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 34-year-old female was struck by a car. You observe asymmetrical chest motion. Following delegation of C-spine control and opening the airway, you should next:
questions.explanation: The signs indicative of chest injury found upon inspection include chest wall contusion, open wounds, subcutaneous emphysema, hemoptysis, distended neck veins, tracheal deviation, asymmetrical chest movement including paradoxical motion, cyanosis, and shock. Initially assisted ventilation with positive pressure.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which one of the following is typically associated with post-traumatic hemorrhage early shock?
options: Loss of 30% to 45% of blood volume
questions.explanation: Early shock is the loss of approximately 15% to 25% of blood volume. That is enough to stimulate slight to moderate tachycardia, pallor, narrowed pulse pressure, thirst, weakness, and possibly delayed capillary refill.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 52-year-old male was struck in the head. Vital signs are BP 134/80, P 88, and R 6 and shallow. You should ventilate to maintain an ETCO2 of:
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient. Maintaining normal ventilation (not hyperventilation) with high-flow oxygen at a rate of about one breath every 6 to 8 seconds (8 to 10 per minute) to maintain an end-tidal CO2 (ETCO2) of 35-45 mmHg.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 52-year-old male has an isolated knee dislocation. He is alert and oriented, BP 144/84, P 88 and R 20. Pulses distal to the location are absent and you have an extended transport time. You should:
options: Splint the injury in the position found
Gently apply traction in an effort realign the extremity to restore pulses
Place patient in a Trendelenburg position
Flex the knee and splint to the opposite leg
questions.explanation: There are certain exceptions to the general rule of splinting injuries in the position found, including when loss of distal pulse is noted. In that case, especially if you have a long transport time to the appropriate hospital, gentle traction should be applied in an effort to bring the injured extremity into a more anatomic position and restore the distal pulse.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Death in the immediate post-burn period is typically associated with:
questions.explanation: Death in the immediate post-burn period is a consequence of associated trauma or conditions such as airway compromise or smoke inhalation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is the most reliable early indicator for determining shock in the injured child?
questions.explanation: Early shock (often referred to as compensated shock) is far more difficult to diagnose in a child than in an adult because of a child's ability to hemodynamically compensate and maintain a normal blood pressure despite life-threatening bleeding. Persistent tachycardia is the most reliable early indicator of shock in a child.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Prevention of which of the following would have the greatest impact for reducing trauma in pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Motor vehicle collisions (MVCs) account for 65% to 75% of pregnancy-related trauma in North America.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When using positive pressure ventilation in a trauma cardiopulmonary arrest patient, you should ventilate:
options: 1 breath every 8 seconds with a tidal volume of 750 cc
12 breaths per minute with a volume of 750 cc
8 breaths per minute with a volume of 500 cc
1 breath every 6 seconds with a volume of 500 cc
questions.explanation: In the TCPA patient, it is reasonable to begin with an assisted ventilation rate of one breath every eight seconds with a tidal volume of 750 cc, which is about eight times per minute using roughly a one-hand squeeze on a standard bag.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The most important factors in determining injuries sustained in a fall are:
options: Distance, impact area on the body, surface struck
Distance, movement during the fall, underlying medical conditions
Distance, underlying medical conditions, surface struck
questions.explanation: The mechanism of injury for falls is vertical deceleration. The types of injuries sustained depend on the following three factors, which you must identify and relay to medical direction: distance of fall; anatomic area impacted; and surface struck.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 34-year-old male is injured in a motor vehicle collision and complains of diffuse abdominal tenderness. The initial assessment reveals fast and shallow respirations, a rapid, weak radial pulse, and pale, cool, diaphoretic skin with no visible external bleeding. You should:
options: Administer oxygen and perform a rapid trauma survey
Immediately evaluate his blood glucose and lactate levels
Complete spinal motion restriction and transport immediately
Obtain SAMPLE history, vital signs and cardiac monitoring
questions.explanation: If you identify a priority (high-risk) patient, you need to find the cause of the abnormal findings to determine if this is a load-and-go patient.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following assessment findings would be an indication for immediate intubation?
questions.explanation: The presence of snoring or stridor or wheezes should alert the rescuer to the presence of airway obstruction. Ultimately, the placement of a 'rescue' ventilation device (BIAD, such as laryngeal mask airway) or endotracheal tube may be required.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unrestrained 17-year-old female driver rear-ends a cement truck at a high rate of speed. She is unconscious, pale, cool and clammy, has fast, labored respirations and fast, thready radial pulses. Her neck veins are flat, trachea midline, chest motion is asymmetrical, and breath sounds on the left are absent. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Clinical signs of shock may be apparent. The neck veins are usually flat secondary to profound hypovolemia, but may very rarely be distended due to mediastinal compression. Other signs of hemothorax include decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion on the affected side.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: The lateral approach for chest decompression is preferred:
options: When the access to the anterior approach is limited or unsafe
To minimize liver or spleen laceration
To help stabilize the catheter during movement or transport
questions.explanation: Military and tactical medical personnel prefer the lateral approach because in a tactical situation it has the advantage of allowing decompression while keeping body armor in place.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is not caused by the release of catecholamines during shock?
questions.explanation: Catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) increase both the rate and strength of the heart's contractions, constrict peripheral arterial blood vessels and affects the sweat glands. Hypotension is the result of the inability to compensate.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What treatment is not indicated in the routine management of the patient with a suspected traumatic brain injury?
options: Maintain a pulse oximetry reading between 94%-99%
Fluid resuscitation to a BP of 110-120 systolic if the patient is hypotensive
Ventilating to obtain an ETCO2 of 35-45 mmHg
Hyperventilating to obtain an ETCO2 of less than 30 mmHg
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation actually has only a slight effect on brain swelling, but causes a significant decrease in cerebral perfusion from that same vasoconstriction, resulting in cerebral hypoxia. Thus, both hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient. Maintaining normal ventilation (not hyperventilation) with high-flow oxygen at a rate of about one breath every 6 to 8 seconds (8 to 10 per minute) to maintain an end-tidal CO2 (ETCO2) of 35-45 mmHg is very important. Prophylactic hyperventilation for TBI is no longer recommended.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Spinal motion restriction should be considered in all of the following except:
options: Blunt trauma with altered level of consciousness
questions.explanation: Immobilization onto a long backboard is not indicated in penetrating wounds of torso, neck, or head unless there is clinical evidence of a spine injury.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which treatment is indicated for the patient who is entrapped and has an isolated crush injury to the lower extremity?
options: Apply tourniquet proximal to the injury
Apply air splint to compress extremity
Locate artery proximal to the wound and apply pressure
questions.explanation: If administration of fluids or medications prior to releasing the entrapped body area is not possible, consider application of a tourniquet proximal to the injury site on the extremity. Although application of the tourniquet will reduce systemic toxin release, crush syndrome will continue to develop.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following best describes how carbon monoxide causes hypoxia?
options: The carbon monoxide atoms bind with hemoglobin and lock out the oxygen
The carbon monoxide atoms block out oxygen in the bloodstream
The carbon monoxide atoms break up the oxygen
The carbon monoxide atoms turn oxygen into carbon dioxide
questions.explanation: Carbon monoxide binds to hemoglobin (257 times stronger than oxygen), resulting in the hemoglobin being unable to transport oxygen. Patients quickly become hypoxic even in the presence of low concentrations of carbon monoxide.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 5-year-old female was struck by a car. She presents with retractions and nasal flaring. The airway is patent, breathing is fast and shallow, and the carotid pulse is weak and slow. You should:
questions.explanation: Note whether the child is 'working' to breathe, demonstrated by subcostal or suprasternal retractions, nasal flaring, or grunting. If you have any doubt that the child is breathing adequately on his or her own, immediately assist the child's breathing.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following statements concerning treatment of shock in the pregnant burn patient is true?
options: Oxygen should be used sparingly so as to avoid oxygen toxicity to the fetus
Vasopressor agents such as dopamine should be used to improve circulation to the fetus
Volume replacement should be given earlier and in larger amounts to the pregnant burn patient
Volume replacement should be given more slowly so as to avoid fluid overloading the fetus
questions.explanation: The overall mortality and morbidity resulting from thermal injuries to the pregnant patient is not markedly different from the non-pregnant patient. However, it is important to remember that the fluid requirement for the pregnant patient is greater than that of the nonpregnant woman.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 17-year-old male is involved in a minor motor vehicle collision and is refusing treatment. The patient has constricted pupils and law enforcement officers on scene suspect drug usage. You suspect the patient ingested:
questions.explanation: Signs and symptoms of narcotic/opiate use are altered mental status, constricted pupils, bradycardia, hypotension, respiratory distress depression and hypothermia.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 49-year-old male was crossing the street and struck by a truck. First responders are doing CPR. The patient is pulseless, apneic and the monitor shows asystole. Your assessment reveals an open skull fracture and deformed pelvis. Based on the ITLS curriculum, which of the following is the most appropriate act?
options: Immediately intubate the patient to secure the airway
Establish intravenous access and administer a 1 liter bolus
Establish intravenous access and administer 2 mg of atropine
Resuscitative efforts should be discontinued, unless prohibited by law
questions.explanation: Resuscitation should be withheld in cases of: (a) blunt trauma with no breathing, pulse, or organized rhythm on ECG on EMS arrival at the scene; (b) penetrating trauma with no breathing, pulse, pupillary reflexes, spontaneous movement, or organized ECG activity; (c) any trauma with injuries obviously incompatible with life (such as decapitation); (d) any trauma with evidence of significant time lapse since pulselessness, including dependent lividity, rigor mortis, etc.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 23-year-old male fell from the roof of a building and reports that he cannot feel his arms or legs. You should first:
options: Apply a cervical collar and transport
Obtain a blood pressure and SAO2
questions.explanation: Once you begin patient assessment in the ITLS Primary Survey, only four things should cause you to interrupt completion of the assessment. You may interrupt the assessment sequence only if (1) the scene becomes unsafe, (2) you must treat exsanguinating hemorrhage, (3) you must treat an airway obstruction, or (4) you must treat cardiac arrest.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When evaluating a patient prior to intubation, you can visualize the upper half of the tonsils. What would you rate the Mallampati score?
questions.explanation: The Mallampati score ranges from I to IV and is dependent on what structures can be viewed when the mouth is opened.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: When performing a needle decompression, which of the following is the correct anatomical location?
options: Directly under the bottom of the second rib, midclavicular line
Directly under the bottom of the third rib, midclavicular line
Directly over the top of the fourth rib, midaxillary line
Directly over the top of the third rib, midclavicular line
questions.explanation: Insert the needle into the second intercostal space at a 90-degree angle to the superior border of the third rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following assessment findings is associated with neurogenic shock?
options: Increased pulse, normal skin color and temperature
Decreased pulse, normal skin color and temperature
questions.explanation: The clinical presentation of neurogenic shock differs from hemorrhagic shock in that there is no catecholamine release and thus no pallor (vasoconstriction), tachycardia, or sweating. The patient will have a decreased blood pressure, but the heart rate will be normal or slow, and the skin is usually warm, dry, and pink.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What treatment is not indicated in the routine management of the adult patient with a head injury?
options: Fluid resuscitation to a BP of 110-120 systolic
Hyperventilating to obtain an ETCO2 of less than 30 mmHg
questions.explanation: Hyperventilation actually has only a slight effect on brain swelling, but causes a significant decrease in cerebral perfusion from that same vasoconstriction, resulting in cerebral hypoxia. Thus, both hyperventilation and hypoventilation can cause cerebral ischemia and increased mortality in the TBI patient. Maintaining normal ventilation (not hyperventilation) with high-flow oxygen at a rate of about one breath every 6 to 8 seconds (8 to 10 per minute) to maintain an end-tidal CO2 (ETCO2) of 35-45 mmHg is very important. Prophylactic hyperventilation for TBI is no longer recommended.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding spinal motion restriction is true?
options: Elderly patients often require padding under the shoulders due to underlying kyphosis.
Smaller children often require more padding under the head because their heads are disproportionately larger.
Elderly patients whose necks have a natural flexed posture will require posterior padding.
Combative patients requiring spinal motion restriction will need to be restrained tighter to ensure that all movement is eliminated.
questions.explanation: Elderly patients whose necks have a naturally flexed posture will require posterior padding.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is not a recommended way to estimate endotracheal tube size for a pediatric patient?
options: Formula: 4 plus the age in years divided by 4
Formula: weight (kg) divided by 5 or weight (lbs.) divided by 10
questions.explanation: There are several ways to choose the right size for the endotracheal tube. The simplest is to refer to your length-based tape system. Other techniques include choosing a tube that is about the same diameter as the tip of the child's little finger or the equation: 4 plus age in years divided by 4.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which of the following is not part of the DOPE mnemonic for evaluating an intubation problem?
options: D - Displacement of the endotracheal tube
questions.explanation: DOPE algorithm: D—displacement of the endotracheal tube, O—obstruction of the endotracheal tube, P—pneumothorax development, or E—equipment problems (ventilator failure, loss of oxygen supply).
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Your first priority in the management of a trauma patient is:
options: Open the airway and assess for breathing
Provide ventilator support for your patient
Begin chest compressions if pulses are absent
questions.explanation: If your patient has major external bleeding, you must immediately direct another team member to control it. As team leader, you may need to initially control the bleeding if no other emergency care provider is immediately available.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Which patient would likely be a difficult laryngoscopy and intubation?
options: Patients with a history of asthma
questions.explanation: MMAP is a technique for predicting when a patient will be difficult to intubate. MMAP stands for Mallampati, measurement 3-3-1 (the 1 being the patient should be able to protrude the lower jaw such that the lower teeth are 1 cm beyond the upper teeth) atlanto-occipital extension, and pathology.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 23-year-old female complains of the inability to feel her legs after falling down a stairwell. Her respirations are rapid and shallow; radial pulses are rapid and weak; and her skin is cool, clammy and ashen with no obvious external bleeding. She has flat neck veins and an unstable pelvis. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Hypovolemic shock victims usually have tachycardia, are pale, and have flat neck veins. So, if you find a trauma victim with a fast heart rate, who is pale, with weak radial pulses and flat neck veins.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unresponsive 27-year-old male struck his head after falling off a bicycle. Vital signs are BP 128/64, P 88, R 18 and GCS 6 (1, 1 & 4). Appropriate care includes all of the following except:
questions.explanation: Endotracheal intubation is still recommended for adults if the airway cannot be maintained or if you cannot maintain adequate oxygenation with supplemental oxygen. There is no reason to routinely intubate patients who are maintaining their airway and have normal oxygen saturation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A confused 37-year-old male removed from a house fire complains of a headache and nausea. There is no evidence of inhalation burns or external trauma. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Headaches and nausea are common symptoms of mild carbon monoxide poisoning, secondary to smoke inhalation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: What is the most commonly injured anatomical area of an infant after a fall from a height?
questions.explanation: Young children who fall from higher than a few feet often land on their heads because the head is disproportionately large and heavy compared to the body of a small child.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: Through apneic oxygenation, a high oxygen concentration gradient may be maintained via:
questions.explanation: Instilling oxygen via a nasal cannula (>8 liters per minute) maintains a high oxygen concentration gradient from the nasopharynx down to the alveoli. As oxygen is absorbed in the alveoli, more oxygen moves down this gradient into the alveoli, even if there is no ventilation.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: An unconscious 18-year-old male is involved in a motor vehicle collision. You find him unrestrained behind the bent steering wheel. He is unconscious with cool, pale and clammy skin. Vitalis are BP 90/40, P 120 and thready and R 30 and shallow. Your assessment reveals that he is blue around the lips, has distended neck veins and tracheal deviation to the right. He also has an asymmetrical chest with absent breath sounds on the left. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Clinical signs of a tension pneumothorax include dyspnea, anxiety, tachypnea, distended neck veins, and possibly tracheal deviation away from the affected side. Auscultation will reveal diminished breath sounds on the affected side and will be accompanied by hyperresonance when percussed.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 16-year-old male sustained a gunshot wound to the left chest and complains of shortness of breath. He presents with rapid and shallow respirations, absent radial pulses, and cool, clammy, pale skin. His neck veins are distended and there is an absence of breath sounds over the left side. You should suspect:
questions.explanation: Any traumatic or medical condition that slows or prevents the venous return of blood can cause shock by lowering cardiac output and thus oxygen delivery to the tissues. Likewise, anything that obstructs the flow of blood to or through the heart can cause shock.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A minimum systolic blood pressure of ______ mmHg is recommended for the adult patient with a traumatic brain injury and a Glasgow Coma Scale of 7.
questions.explanation: ITLS advocates maintaining systolic blood pressure of at least 110 mmHg in the TBI patient to ensure adequate perfusion, which is higher than the Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines but consistent with those put forth by Arizona EPIC.
questions.subject: Trauma

questions.text: A 7-year-old boy has difficulty breathing and is wheezing. Vital signs are BP 94/62, HR 128, and RR 32. What initial management should be performed?
options: Open the airway with the jaw-thrust maneuver.
Place the patient in the Trendelenburg position.
questions.explanation: The child is in respiratory distress with wheezing but has adequate ventilation and perfusion. The first step is to provide high-flow oxygen via nonrebreather mask to correct hypoxia. Jaw-thrust is used for trauma or unconscious patients; bag-mask ventilation is for inadequate ventilatory effort; Trendelenburg is not indicated in respiratory distress.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 4-month-old infant who has had vomiting and diarrhea for the past 36 hours is lethargic, has a weak cry, and has mottled skin color. Vital signs are BP 62/46, HR 172, and RR 48. What do the signs and symptoms suggest?
questions.explanation: The history of significant fluid loss (vomiting/diarrhea), tachycardia, hypotension, and signs of poor perfusion (lethargy, mottling, weak cry) are classic for hypovolemic shock. Respiratory arrest would present with bradypnea or apnea; meningitis typically includes fever; cardiogenic shock usually arises from a known cardiac condition.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the priority treatment for a child who is seizing?
questions.explanation: The immediate priority in any seizure is ensuring a patent airway, as the tongue may obstruct or secretions may accumulate. Opening the airway takes precedence over gathering history, spinal precautions (unless trauma is suspected), or inserting an adjunct.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What should be included in the treatment of thermal burns to the chest, abdomen, and legs?
options: Apply burn cream to burned areas.
Apply wet dressings to the burned areas.
Place the patient in the shock position.
questions.explanation: For significant burns, administration of 100% oxygen is indicated due to the risk of inhalation injury, carbon monoxide exposure, and increased metabolic demand. Burn creams, wet dressings, and the shock position are not standard prehospital interventions for burn patients.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 2-year-old boy, who is alert and crying, has multiple lacerations and a deformed lower leg after being hit by a car. Additional assessment reveals that he has warm, dry, pink skin and bruising on the lateral right lower chest. What is the significance of the bruising to the right side of his chest?
options: The ribs are pliable, which makes organs more susceptible to underlying damage.
The chest wall muscles absorb the energy of impact and protect underlying organs from injury.
Rib fractures may be present because the chest wall is thin.
The intercostal muscles may be damaged because they are not well developed.
questions.explanation: Young children have highly pliable ribs that often do not fracture. Therefore, force applied to the chest is transmitted directly to underlying structures (lung, liver) causing pulmonary contusion, pneumothorax, or solid organ injury, even without rib fractures. Bruising should raise suspicion for significant intrathoracic or intra-abdominal injury.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 9-year-old patient fell down a steep incline after he rolled over the handlebars of his bicycle. He was not wearing a helmet and has begun vomiting. How should you manage this patient?
questions.explanation: Vomiting in a patient with a possible head and spine injury poses a serious aspiration risk. The first step is to immediately suction the airway to clear vomitus and maintain patency. Once the airway is clear, cervical spine stabilization and oxygen administration should follow. Elevating the head is contraindicated before spinal assessment.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following patients may have injuries that suggest maltreatment?
options: A 4-year-old with bilateral bruises to the shins.
A 6-year-old with bruises throughout the back.
An 8-year-old with a laceration on the face.
A 10-year-old with several abrasions on the forearm.
questions.explanation: Bruises on the back are atypical for accidental injury and should raise concern for physical abuse. Bruises on the shins are common with normal play; facial lacerations and forearm abrasions are frequent in childhood falls and activities.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: The history provided by the caregiver of which of the following patients is inconsistent with the injury?
options: A 9-month-old infant with a bump on the head from falling out of a highchair reaching for a toy.
A 2½-year-old girl with a facial laceration from pulling a toaster off a counter.
A 2-year-old boy with several small, healing circular burns from running into a lit cigarette.
A 4-year-old boy with a wrist deformity from falling off a swing set at the park.
questions.explanation: Well-circumscribed, circular, healing burns are characteristic of cigarette burns and are highly suspicious for inflicted injury. A story of a toddler 'running into a lit cigarette' is implausible. The other scenarios describe mechanisms that match the injury pattern.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A newborn who was just delivered at home has cyanosis of the hands and feet, has a strong cry, and is moving her extremities vigorously. Vital signs are HR 164 and RR 52. What is the first step in managing this patient?
options: Dry her and then wrap her in a blanket.
Ventilate her with a bag-mask device.
Give high-flow oxygen by nonrebreather mask.
Maintain her airway using the jaw-thrust maneuver.
questions.explanation: Acrocyanosis (bluish hands and feet) is normal in a newborn. The infant has a strong cry, normal heart rate, and good respiratory effort, indicating no need for resuscitation. The priority is to prevent heat loss by drying and wrapping the baby in a warm blanket.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the best way to obtain information about the medical history of a 6-year-old girl who is on a ventilator and has a tracheostomy?
options: Contact the hospital where she is usually admitted.
questions.explanation: Family caregivers are the most direct and knowledgeable source of the child's baseline status, equipment settings, and medical history. They are immediately available and can provide critical information for emergency care.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: In what position should the child in mild respiratory distress be placed?
options: On his or her right side.
questions.explanation: Allowing the child to assume a position of comfort optimizes breathing and reduces anxiety. Forcing a specific position may increase respiratory effort and distress.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: An 8-year-old boy has been sick for 3 days. He has been experiencing nausea and diarrhea that has become progressively worse. He is semi-responsive with pale and diaphoretic skin. It is difficult to obtain a BP. Other vital signs are HR 54 and RR 10. What type of shock is this patient experiencing?
questions.explanation: Prolonged vomiting and diarrhea lead to fluid loss and hypovolemic shock. The bradycardia (HR 54) is a preterminal sign of decompensated shock in children. There is no history of heart disease (cardiogenic), fever (septic), or spinal injury (neurogenic).
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: An unrestrained 4-year-old girl was ejected during a two-vehicle motor vehicle collision. She is crying vigorously while moving her upper extremities. Her lower extremities appear deformed. What is the appropriate initial management of this patient?
options: Initiate immobilization of the lower extremities.
Ventilate her with a bag-mask device.
Cover her with a blanket to keep her warm.
questions.explanation: A significant mechanism (ejection from vehicle) requires immediate cervical spine immobilization to prevent further spinal injury. The child is moving and crying, indicating spontaneous ventilation and sufficient airway protection. Management of extremity deformities and warmth are secondary priorities.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the most common cause of pediatric seizures?
questions.explanation: Febrile seizures are the most common type of seizures in children, typically occurring between 6 months and 5 years of age. Head trauma, epilepsy, and brain tumors are less common causes.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: When is it appropriate to use an AED in a witnessed pediatric cardiac arrest?
options: Once the patient is in the ambulance.
When the ambulance is en route to the hospital.
questions.explanation: Early defibrillation is a key link in the chain of survival. For a witnessed pediatric cardiac arrest, the AED should be applied and used as soon as it is available. Delaying defibrillation reduces the chance of successful resuscitation.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 7-year-old boy was having difficulty breathing during his physical education class. He appears tired and has audible wheezing. His vital signs are BP 98/66, HR 124 beats/min, and RR 32 breaths/min. What should the treatment of this patient include?
options: Initiating bag-mask ventilation at 12 breaths per minute.
questions.explanation: The presentation is consistent with an acute asthma exacerbation. The child is tachypneic and wheezing but still maintaining adequate ventilation (no respiratory failure). The first-line treatment is a nebulized beta-agonist (e.g., albuterol) to reverse bronchospasm. Intubation and bag-mask ventilation are reserved for respiratory failure; epinephrine is indicated for anaphylaxis.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the first step in caring for a patient with a tracheostomy tube who is having difficulty breathing?
options: Administer a nebulizer treatment with a beta-agonist medication.
Place a mask over the mouth and initiate bag-mask ventilation.
questions.explanation: The most common cause of acute respiratory distress in a patient with a tracheostomy is a mucous plug. The first step is to suction the tracheostomy tube to clear the obstruction. If no improvement, further interventions (tube replacement, ventilation via mouth) may be considered.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 3-month-old infant is found unresponsive in his crib and has cool, mottled skin. His vital signs are HR 50 and RR 6. What is the initial treatment for this patient?
options: Establish IV access and administer an IV fluid bolus of 20 mL/kg.
Establish IV access and administer 0.01 mg/kg IV epinephrine 1:10,000.
Perform chest compressions and ventilate with a bag-mask device.
Initiate bag-mask ventilation and then reassess his skin color and heart rate.
questions.explanation: An infant with a heart rate less than 60 bpm and signs of poor perfusion requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, starting with chest compressions and ventilations. Ventilation alone may be insufficient; bradycardia with poor perfusion is treated as a cardiac arrest.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following is an indication for insertion of an intraosseous needle?
options: You are unable to obtain peripheral access within 90 seconds in a 2-year-old child in impending arrest.
You have just arrived on scene to find a 3-month-old infant who has pink skin color and is lethargic following a seizure.
You have attempted all peripheral access sites in a 7-year-old child in respiratory failure.
The transport time is longer than 10 minutes for a 4-year-old boy in hypovolemic shock.
questions.explanation: IO access is indicated when peripheral IV access cannot be rapidly established in a critically ill or injured child (e.g., impending arrest). A 90-second timeframe is a common guideline. Exhausting all peripheral sites before IO insertion delays necessary treatment. Transport time alone is not an indication.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 4-year-old boy fell down a 15-foot embankment and landed on packed dirt. He is unresponsive and has pale, cool skin and a capillary refill time of 4 seconds. Additional assessment reveals a 2 cm laceration on the left side of the forehead with minimal bleeding and a distended abdomen. His vital signs are BP 78/56, HR 146, and RR 48. What are the initial management steps for this patient?
options: Administer oxygen by nasal cannula, followed by a detailed physical exam before transport.
Manage blood loss from the forehead, establish IV access and give a slow infusion of fluids, and prepare for transport.
Administer oxygen by nonrebreather mask, establish intraosseous access and give a 10 mL/kg bolus, and place him in a high Fowler's position.
Maintain cervical spine stabilization, establish IV access and give a 20 mL/kg bolus, complete the spinal immobilization, and initiate transport.
questions.explanation: This child has a significant mechanism of injury, signs of shock (hypotension, tachycardia, delayed capillary refill), and a distended abdomen suggesting internal hemorrhage. Priorities are spinal motion restriction, vascular access, fluid resuscitation with an initial 20 mL/kg bolus, full spinal immobilization, and rapid transport. High-flow oxygen is indicated, but a nonrebreather mask, not nasal cannula, is appropriate. A detailed exam on scene delays transport.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the dysrhythmia for a 3-year-old with a heart rate of 180 beats/min, a narrow QRS complex, and a variable R-R interval called?
questions.explanation: Sinus tachycardia in a 3-year-old has a heart rate usually less than 220 bpm, a narrow QRS, and often some beat-to-beat variability (sinus arrhythmia). SVT typically has a rate exceeding 220 bpm, is abrupt in onset, and has a fixed R-R interval. Ventricular tachycardia has a wide QRS.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the initial airway management of a child who is seizing and has gurgling respirations?
questions.explanation: Gurgling respirations indicate the presence of secretions or vomitus in the upper airway. The first step is to suction the oropharynx to clear the airway. Insertion of an adjunct or bag-mask ventilation may be necessary after suctioning if the airway remains compromised.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 3-year-old boy reportedly fell down a flight of stairs and has a swollen, deformed right forearm. You also note several small, healing, circular burns on his arms and back. He appears quiet and withdrawn. What is the treatment of this patient?
questions.explanation: The immediate medical priority is to stabilize the acute orthopedic injury by splinting the deformed forearm. While the burns raise suspicion for abuse and must be reported, the question asks for the treatment intervention. Covering the burns or establishing IV access is not urgent; law enforcement notification is a part of overall management but not the primary treatment action.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the best way to repair a break in a central line catheter?
options: Place a piece of silk tape horizontally over the break.
Clamp the catheter with a hemostat and gauze.
questions.explanation: If a central line catheter breaks, the catheter should be clamped between the break and the patient (using a padded hemostat) to prevent air embolism or bleeding. The catheter is never removed in the field, and irrigation can introduce air. Taping is insufficient to occlude the lumen.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is a contraindication to straightening the head and neck of a trauma patient during spinal immobilization?
questions.explanation: If resistance to movement is encountered or the patient complains of pain when attempting to align the head and neck into a neutral position, forced straightening could cause further spinal injury. The cervical spine should be immobilized in the position found. Facial injuries, dilated pupils, and headache do not contraindicate gentle alignment.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 4-year-old semi-responsive boy has had difficulty breathing and a fever for 3 days. Vital signs are BP 60/40, HR 128, and RR 10. What initial management should be performed?
options: Open the airway and maintain cervical spine immobilization.
Open the airway and ventilate with a bag-mask device.
Place the patient in the Trendelenburg position.
Apply oxygen by nonrebreather mask at 15 L/min.
questions.explanation: The child is in respiratory failure (bradypnea, hypotension, altered mental status). The priority is to open the airway and provide immediate positive pressure ventilation with a bag-mask device. Oxygen via nonrebreather mask is inadequate for inadequate ventilation; cervical spine immobilization is not indicated without trauma; Trendelenburg does not assist breathing.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 12-year-old boy was shot in the abdomen during a hunting accident. He has altered mental status and his skin is cool and clammy. Vital signs are BP 64/46, weak HR 142, and RR 38. What do the signs and symptoms suggest?
questions.explanation: Penetrating abdominal trauma with hypotension, tachycardia, and altered mental status indicates hypovolemic shock from internal hemorrhage. There are no signs of tension pneumothorax (tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds), neurogenic shock (spinal injury, bradycardia), or cardiac tamponade (muffled heart sounds, JVD).
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the priority treatment for a child with altered mental status?
questions.explanation: ABCs take precedence. A child with altered mental status is at risk of airway obstruction from the tongue or secretions; opening the airway is the initial critical action. History, glucose check, and spinal precautions follow.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 4-year-old boy is screaming and crying after he fell into a campfire. He has partial- and full-thickness burns on both arms. Vital signs are HR 140, BP 100/70, and RR 40. What should be done first in the treatment of thermal burns?
options: Apply burn cream to burned areas.
Remove any clothing that is still smoldering.
Apply moist dressings to all of the burned areas.
Place the patient in the shock position.
questions.explanation: The first step is to stop the burning process by removing any smoldering or hot clothing. Burn creams and moist dressings are not standard prehospital interventions; the shock position is not indicated for burns.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 2-year-old boy, who is alert and crying, has multiple lacerations and a deformed forearm after he was ejected from a vehicle. Additional assessment reveals that he has cool, moist, pale skin and bruising on the lateral right lower chest. What is the significance of the bruising to the right side of his chest?
options: The ribs are pliable, which makes organs more susceptible to underlying damage.
The chest wall muscles absorb the energy of impact and protect underlying organs from injury.
Rib fractures may be present because the chest wall is thin.
The intercostal muscles may be damaged because they are not well developed.
questions.explanation: Young children have pliable ribs that often do not fracture. Force applied to the chest is transmitted directly to underlying structures (lung, liver), causing contusions, pneumothorax, or solid organ injury. Bruising should raise suspicion for significant internal injury even without rib fractures.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 7-year-old semi-conscious patient rolled off of a 4-foot embankment when he fell off of his father's all-terrain vehicle. He was not wearing a helmet and has begun vomiting. How should you manage this patient?
questions.explanation: Vomiting in a patient with altered mental status and possible head/spine injury poses a high aspiration risk. Immediate suctioning of the airway is the priority. Elevating the head is contraindicated before spinal assessment. Finger sweep is only for visible solid objects; NPA may follow if needed.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A newborn who was just delivered at home has cyanosis of the hands and feet, a weak cry, and vigorous movement of the extremities. Vital signs are HR 86 and RR 52. What is the most important step in managing this patient?
options: Dry her and then wrap her in a blanket.
Ventilate her with a bag-mask device.
Give high-flow oxygen by a blow-by device.
Maintain her airway using the jaw-thrust maneuver.
questions.explanation: A heart rate below 100 bpm in a newborn indicates inadequate oxygenation and the need for positive pressure ventilation (NRP guidelines). Even though acrocyanosis is normal, the weak cry and bradycardia require immediate bag-mask ventilation. Drying/warming is not sufficient.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 2-year-old girl has been having respiratory distress for about an hour. She has crackles noted in her lower lobes and her lips are cyanotic. She is semi-responsive with pale and diaphoretic skin. It is difficult to obtain a BP. Other vital signs are HR 270 and RR 60. What type of shock is this patient experiencing?
questions.explanation: The extreme tachycardia (HR 270) is most consistent with supraventricular tachycardia, not sinus tachycardia. Combined with crackles and respiratory distress, this suggests heart failure due to a primary arrhythmia, causing cardiogenic shock. No history of fluid loss, infection, or spinal trauma exists.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: An 8-year-old girl fell from a tree house that is approximately 10 feet from the ground. She is screaming and crying due to a deformity of her right lower leg. What is the appropriate initial management of this patient?
options: Consider preparing the AED and applying pads.
Cover her with a blanket and apply heat packs.
questions.explanation: A significant mechanism (fall from height) mandates immediate cervical spine immobilization to prevent further injury. The child is alert and crying, so airway/ventilation is adequate. Extremity immobilization is secondary.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: After a seizure, patients are in a lethargic and confused state. What is this state known as?
questions.explanation: The postictal state is the period of altered consciousness that follows a seizure.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the appropriate compression depth in a 6-month-old child in cardiac arrest?
questions.explanation: Per AHA guidelines, the recommended compression depth for an infant (under 1 year) is approximately 1.5 inches (4 cm), which is one-third the anterior-posterior diameter of the chest.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 2-year-old girl has been sick for 2 days. On arrival, she has a barklike cough and is extremely lethargic. Her vital signs are BP 66/P, HR 90, and RR 58. What should the treatment include for this patient?
options: Initiating bag-mask ventilation at 12 beats per minute.
Performing deep suction utilizing soft suction tubing.
questions.explanation: A barklike cough, lethargy, and hypotension suggest severe croup (laryngotracheobronchitis). Nebulized epinephrine reduces subglottic airway edema. Beta-agonists treat bronchospasm (asthma), not upper airway edema. Suctioning can worsen swelling; bag-mask ventilation would follow if respiratory failure progresses.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 3-year-old boy with a tracheostomy is cyanotic and anxious after a recent tracheostomy change by a new home health nurse. After suctioning and attempting to ventilate with a bag, what should the provider consider?
options: Place a mask over the mouth and initiate bag-mask ventilation.
questions.explanation: If suctioning fails to clear the tracheostomy tube and ventilation remains ineffective, the tube may be obstructed or misplaced. Replacing the tracheostomy tube is the next step. Suctioning the oropharynx or ventilating via the mouth does not address a tracheostomy problem.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: After delivery of an unresponsive female newborn, you note she has central cyanosis and vital signs of HR 50 and RR 6. What is your initial treatment?
options: Establish IV access and administer an IV fluid bolus of 20 mL/kg.
Establish IV access and administer 0.01 mg/kg IV epinephrine 1:10,000.
Perform chest compressions and ventilate with a bag-mask device.
Initiate bag-mask ventilation and then reassess skin color and heart rate.
questions.explanation: A newborn with a heart rate below 60 bpm and apnea requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation (chest compressions coordinated with bag-mask ventilation), per NRP guidelines. Ventilation alone is insufficient for severe bradycardia.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following would NOT be considered an approved site for intraosseous insertion?
questions.explanation: The sternum is not a standard pediatric IO site. Approved sites in children include the distal femur, proximal tibia, and humerus. (Sternal IO may be used in adults but is not indicated in pediatrics.)
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 12-year-old boy fell off of his dirt bike. He is unresponsive with pale, cool skin and has a capillary refill time of 4 seconds. Additional assessment reveals a deformed right forearm and a distended abdomen. His vital signs are BP 78/56, HR 146, and RR 48. What are the initial management steps for this patient?
options: Administer oxygen by nasal cannula, followed by a detailed physical exam before transport.
Stabilize the forearm, establish IV access and give a slow infusion of fluids, and prepare for transport.
Administer oxygen by nonrebreather mask, establish intraosseous access and give a 10 mL/kg bolus, and place him in a high Fowler's position.
Maintain cervical spine stabilization, establish IV access and give a 20 mL/kg bolus, complete the spinal immobilization, and initiate transport.
questions.explanation: This patient has a significant mechanism, signs of shock (hypotension, tachycardia, delayed cap refill), and a distended abdomen suggesting internal hemorrhage. Priorities are spinal motion restriction, vascular access, fluid resuscitation with an initial 20 mL/kg bolus, full spinal immobilization, and rapid transport. High-flow oxygen via nonrebreather mask is indicated (not nasal cannula). Scene time should be minimized.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the dysrhythmia for a 16-year-old with a heart rate of 180 beats/min, a narrow QRS complex, and a nonvariable RR interval?
questions.explanation: A rate of 180, narrow QRS, and a regular (nonvariable) R-R interval is classic for supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). Sinus tachycardia usually has some beat-to-beat variability and rates below 220 in adolescents. Ventricular tachycardia has a wide QRS.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the initial airway management of an unrestrained child in a motor vehicle collision who has blood and broken teeth noted in the airway along with gurgling respirations?
questions.explanation: Gurgling respirations indicate liquid or debris in the upper airway. The first step is suctioning to clear blood and foreign material and establish a patent airway.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: An 18-month-old boy reportedly fell down a flight of stairs and has a swollen, deformed right forearm. You also note several small, healing, circular bruises on his legs and back. What is the initial treatment of this patient?
questions.explanation: The immediate medical priority is to stabilize the acute orthopedic injury by splinting the deformed forearm. While the circular bruises are suspicious for abuse and must be reported, the question asks for the treatment intervention. IV access or covering the lesions is not urgent.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 5-year-old boy is wheezing and complains of chest tightness. He is sitting upright, using accessory muscles to breathe. Vital signs are BP 92/62, HR 128, and RR 36, at oxygen saturation in 93%. What Initial management should be performed?
options: Open the airway with the jaw thrust maneuver.
questions.explanation: Initial management includes oxygen to correct hypoxia. Definitive treatment requires bronchodilators (e.g., nebulized beta-agonists).
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following is an acceptable means of safe transport for a child in an ambulance?
options: A child held in a parent's arms with the parent secured to the stretcher.
A child in an age-appropriate car seat secured via two straps to the stretcher.
The child transported in his own car seat after a rollover motor vehicle accident.
The child belted in the captain's chair in the ambulance.
questions.explanation: The safest method is an age-appropriate car seat secured to the ambulance stretcher with at least two straps.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 3-year-old boy has been seizing for approximately 2 minutes and has gurgling sounds. Which of the following is the first priority?
options: Administer midazolam 0.2mg/kg by the intranasal route.
questions.explanation: Gurgling sounds indicate secretions in the airway. Suctioning takes priority to maintain patency. If no gurgling were present, airway positioning would be first.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 9-year-old boy is screaming and crying after he fell into a backyard bonfire. He has full-thickness burns on both arms and legs. His hair and eyebrows are singed. Vital signs are HR 140, BP 100/70, and RR 30. What should be done first?
options: Apply burn cream to burned areas.
Apply a wet sheet dressing to the burned areas.
Place the patient in the shock position.
questions.explanation: Singed hair and eyebrows indicate potential inhalation injury; high-flow 100% oxygen is the priority.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 10-year-old cyclist without a helmet fell from her bicycle while riding downhill. She is confused and has vomited twice. Which of the following is an appropriate management?
options: Open the airway with a head-tilt/chin-lift maneuver.
questions.explanation: Vomiting with altered mental status and potential head injury poses an aspiration risk. Suctioning is the priority.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following patients has a potential child abuse injury?
options: A 4-year-old with bilateral bruises to the shins.
A 6-year-old with bruises throughout the back.
An 8-year-old with a laceration on the face.
A 10-year-old with several abrasions on the forearm.
questions.explanation: Bruises on the back are highly suspicious for physical abuse; bruises on shins, facial lacerations, and forearm abrasions are common in normal play.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: High pitched respiratory sound associated with airway constriction?
questions.explanation: Stridor is a high-pitched sound caused by obstruction of the upper airway. Wheezing is associated with lower airway constriction.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following findings is the first to present when a child is in a state of compensated shock?
questions.explanation: Tachycardia is the earliest and most consistent sign of compensated shock in children.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 2-year-old boy has moderate respiratory distress. He appears uncomfortable and has a respiratory rate of 28 breaths per minute with tracheal tugging and stridor. Which of the following is the best position for the patient?
questions.explanation: Allowing the child to assume a position of comfort optimizes airway patency and reduces anxiety.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 12-year-old boy with autism is behaving aggressively at school. He has thrown a chair at his teacher and attempted to bite a classmate. Which of the following is an appropriate initial step in caring for this patient?
options: Speak directly to the boy and ask him to cooperate with you.
Distract the boy and have your partner restrain him from behind.
Ask the police to place him in handcuffs before you begin your assessment.
questions.explanation: Verbal de-escalation is the least restrictive and most appropriate first step before considering physical restraint.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A baby has just been delivered at home. She is crying and breathing but her feet are blue. After clamping and cutting the cord, what do you do next?
options: Intubate her with a bag-mask device.
Maintain her airway using the jaw-thrust maneuver.
questions.explanation: Acrocyanosis is normal; the crying, breathing baby needs to be dried and swaddled to prevent heat loss.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 6-year-old child was run over by a lawnmower. He has a significant amount of bleeding from the lower extremity. Despite the application of direct pressure with gauze, the bleeding continues. His vital signs include HR 160, RR 24 (crying), and BP 90/61. What is the highest priority?
options: Provide supplemental oxygen at 10 liters per minute by non-rebreather mask.
Apply a tourniquet to the right thigh.
questions.explanation: Life-threatening hemorrhage uncontrolled by direct pressure requires immediate application of a tourniquet proximal to the bleeding site.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following is the best description of optimal ventilation volume for a 6-month-old child?
options: Ventilate until you see abdominal rise.
Ventilate just until the chest begins to rise.
questions.explanation: Ventilate with just enough volume to cause visible chest rise to avoid gastric distention and barotrauma.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: An 8-year-old boy has been sick for 4 days with vomiting and diarrhea. He has not tolerated any fluids for the past 2 days. He is disoriented and pale. His HR is 162 and RR is 12, and BP is unstable to obtain. What type of shock is this?
questions.explanation: Prolonged vomiting, diarrhea, and poor fluid intake lead to hypovolemic shock. Tachycardia, altered mental status, and unmeasurable BP indicate decompensated hypovolemic shock.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 4-year-old boy was in a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He is lying on the ground and has no obvious injuries. What is the most appropriate first step in management?
options: Immobilize him on a long spine board.
Provide supplemental oxygen at 4 liters per minute by nasal cannula.
Remove his clothing to look for other injuries.
questions.explanation: A high-speed mechanism requires immediate spinal motion restriction. The first practical step among the options is placement of a cervical collar, while manual stabilization is performed simultaneously.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: In which of the following situations would activated charcoal be contraindicated in a case of ingestion?
options: Child is younger than age 1 year.
questions.explanation: Activated charcoal is contraindicated in patients with altered mental status due to the risk of aspiration.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: Which of the following signs suggests that an intraosseous (IO) line is not in proper placement?
options: Bubbles of blood appear when pulling back from the line.
There is swelling of the soft tissue around the site.
Fluid requires pressure to flow into the line.
The needle comes out from the tibia at an angle.
questions.explanation: Swelling around the IO site indicates extravasation of fluid into soft tissues, suggesting improper placement.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the 1st thing you do when a kid with a tracheostomy is in respiratory distress and his ventilator alarms are going off?
questions.explanation: Disconnect from the ventilator and immediately begin manual ventilation via the tracheostomy tube.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A child has a heart rate between 180 - 188 beats/min with a narrow QRS and a strong pulse. Which of the following best describes his rhythm?
questions.explanation: In children, sinus tachycardia can reach rates of ~180–200 bpm. A rate of 180–188 bpm with a strong pulse may still represent sinus tachycardia unless it is very regular, abrupt in onset, and lacks variability, which would suggest SVT.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What medication can be administered intranasal?
questions.explanation: Midazolam is commonly administered intranasally for seizure control.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 2-year-old boy, who is alert and crying, has multiple lacerations and a deformed lower leg after he was struck by a car. Additional assessment reveals that he has warm, dry, pink skin and bruising on the lateral right lower chest. What is the significance of the bruising to the right side of his chest?
options: The ribs are pliable, which makes organs more susceptible to underlying damage.
The chest wall muscles absorb the energy of impact and protect underlying organs from injury.
Rib fractures may be present because the chest wall is thin.
The intercostal muscles may be damaged because they are not well developed.
questions.explanation: Children have pliable ribs that transmit force to internal organs without fracturing. Therefore, chest wall bruising may indicate significant underlying injury (e.g., pulmonary contusion or liver injury) even in the absence of rib fractures.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A newborn who was just delivered at home has cyanosis of the hands and feet, a strong cry, and vigorous movement of the extremities. Vital signs are HR 120 and RR 52. What is the first step in managing this patient?
options: Dry her and then wrap her in a blanket.
Ventilate her with a bag-mask device.
Give high-flow oxygen by nonrebreather mask.
Maintain her airway using the jaw-thrust maneuver.
questions.explanation: Heart rate above 100 bpm, strong cry, and good tone indicate a well-transitioning newborn. The first step is to dry and warm the infant to prevent heat loss.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the dysrhythmia for a 16-year-old with a heart rate of 180 beats/min, a narrow QRS complex, and a non-variable RR interval?
questions.explanation: A narrow-complex tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no variability suggests SVT. Sinus tachycardia typically shows variability and is associated with an underlying cause (e.g., fever, dehydration).
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 5 year old boy has wheezing and looks uncomfortable. What is the appropriate treatment?
questions.explanation: Initial management includes supplemental oxygen. However, wheezing indicates bronchospasm, and definitive treatment requires bronchodilators (e.g., nebulized beta-agonists), which should be administered as soon as possible.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is the correct way to safely transport a child in the ambulance?
options: In an age appropriate car seat secured to the stretcher with 2 straps
questions.explanation: The safest method is an age-appropriate car seat secured to the ambulance stretcher with at least two straps.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 3 year old boy has been seizing for approximately 2 minutes. What is the first priority for treatment?
questions.explanation: The first priority during a seizure is airway protection. Positioning and clearing the airway (including suction if secretions are present) help prevent aspiration.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 9 year old boy is screaming and crying after falling into a backyard bonfire. He has full and partial thickness burns on his arms and legs his hair and eyebrows are singed. His vitals are as follows: HR 140, BP 101/70, RR 36. What would be an appropriate first treatment?
questions.explanation: Singed hair and eyebrows indicate potential inhalation injury. High-flow 100% oxygen is indicated for possible carbon monoxide exposure and to support oxygenation.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: High pitched respiratory sound on expiration is best described as?
questions.explanation: Wheezing is a high-pitched musical sound heard primarily on expiration, caused by narrowing of the lower airways.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is one of the 1st signs of compensated shock in children?
questions.explanation: Tachycardia is one of the earliest signs of compensated shock in children.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A 9 year old looks uncomfortable with accessory muscle use and moderate trouble breathing. What position should you transport him in?
questions.explanation: Allowing a child in respiratory distress to assume a position of comfort optimizes ventilation mechanics and minimizes anxiety.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A newborn comes out with blue extremities but is breathing and has a heart beat. What is your first step?
questions.explanation: Acrocyanosis (blue hands/feet) in a breathing newborn is normal. The first step is to dry and swaddle the baby to prevent heat loss.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: When is activated charcoal contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Activated charcoal is contraindicated in patients with altered level of consciousness due to the risk of aspiration.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A girl gets her foot caught in a lawnmower and suffers a near amputation injury to her lower leg. She is bleeding profusely even after applying a pressure dressing. What should you do next?
options: Apply a tourniquet on the calf
questions.explanation: Apply a tourniquet proximal to the injury, 5–7 cm above the wound, avoiding joints.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: What is a sign of an improperly placed IO?
questions.explanation: Swelling around the IO site indicates extravasation of fluid into soft tissues, suggesting improper placement or dislodgement.
questions.subject: Pediatrics

questions.text: A patient presents with a recent onset of shortness of breath with exertion. She is presently receiving chemotherapy for treatment of breast cancer. Your physical exam reveals jugular venous distention and muffled heart sounds. Of the following, the most likely etiology of these findings is:
questions.explanation: Beck's triad (JVD, muffled heart tones, hypotension) is classic for cardiac tamponade. Chemotherapy (especially with certain agents) can cause pericardial effusion leading to tamponade. Pneumothorax would have absent breath sounds; pulmonary embolism presents with sudden dyspnea/pleuritic pain; pericarditis often has chest pain worse lying flat.
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: Your patient is a 20-year-old male complaining of a two-day history of diffuse abdominal pain that today, localized in his lower right quadrant. This is most likely caused by:
questions.explanation: Appendicitis often initially presents with periumbilical pain that transitions into right lower quadrant pain (McBurney's point). Cholecystitis is right upper quadrant; diverticulitis is left lower quadrant (elderly); pancreatitis is epigastric radiating to back.
questions.hint: Pain migration to RLQ ? classic appendix.
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: A patient in a nursing home is responsive only to painful stimulus. Nursing staff states the patient has a history of diabetes and a stroke. The staff reports that another patient in the same room is also experiencing an acute onset of altered level of consciousness. Which of the following should you do first?
options: Lift the patient's arm to see if one drifts.
Ask if anyone else has the same symptoms.
questions.explanation: When more than one person presents with the same signs/symptoms, suspect an environmental cause (e.g., carbon monoxide poisoning, toxic exposure). Asking about others is the priority for scene safety and mass casualty considerations. Blood glucose (B) would be done later. Stroke assessment (A) is not first. Narcan (D) only if opiate overdose suspected.
questions.hint: Multiple patients affected ? think scene/environment first.
questions.subject: Medical/Behavioral/Geriatric

questions.text: Which of the following techniques could help you better communicate with a sitting patient who has difficulty hearing?
